name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ سَيَقُولُ ٱلسُّفَهَآءُ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَا وَلَّىٰهُمْ عَن قِبْلَتِهِمُ ٱلَّتِى كَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهَا ۚ قُل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٤٢)
```

```english
The foolish among the people will say, ‘What has turned them away from the qiblah they were following?’ Say, ‘To Allah belong the east and the west. He guides whomever He wishes to a straight path.’ (142)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا لِّتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا ۗ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلْقِبْلَةَ ٱلَّتِى كُنتَ عَلَيْهَآ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يَتَّبِعُ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِمَّن يَنقَلِبُ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ ۚ وَإِن كَانَتْ لَكَبِيرَةً إِلَّا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَـٰنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِٱلنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٤٣)
```

```english
Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you. We did not appoint the qiblah you were following, but that We may ascertain those who follow the Apostle from those who turn back on their heels. It was indeed a hard thing except for those whom Allah has guided. And Allah would not let your prayers go to waste. Indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to mankind. (143)
```

```arabic
قَدْ نَرَىٰ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَىٰهَا ۚ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَحَيْثُ مَا كُنتُمْ فَوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُۥ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٤)
```

```english
We certainly see you turning your face about in the sky. We will surely turn you to a qiblah of your liking: so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it! Indeed those who were given the Book surely know that it is the truth from their Lord. And Allah is not oblivious of what they do. (144)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِنْ أَتَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِكُلِّ ءَايَةٍ مَّا تَبِعُوا۟ قِبْلَتَكَ ۚ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَتَهُمْ ۚ وَمَا بَعْضُهُم بِتَابِعٍ قِبْلَةَ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۙ إِنَّكَ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٥)
```

```english
Even if you bring those who were given the Book every [kind of] sign, they will not follow your qiblah. Nor shall you follow their qiblah, nor will any of them follow the qiblah of the other. And if you follow their desires, after the knowledge that has come to you, you will be one of the wrongdoers. (145)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَعْرِفُونَهُۥ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنْهُمْ لَيَكْتُمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٤٦)
```

```english
Those whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their sons, but a part of them indeed conceal the truth while they know. (146)
```

```arabic
ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ فَلَا تَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ (١٤٧)
```

```english
This is the truth from your Lord; so do not be among the skeptics. (147)
```

```arabic
وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا۟ يَأْتِ بِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٤٨)
```

```english
Everyone has a cynosure to which he turns; so take the lead in all good works. Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (148)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٩)
```

```english
Whencesoever you may go out, turn your face towards the Holy Mosque. Indeed it is the truth from your Lord, and Allah is not oblivious of what you do. (149)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ حَيْثُ خَرَجْتَ فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ۚ وَحَيْثُ مَا كُنتُمْ فَوَلُّوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُۥ لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَيْكُمْ حُجَّةٌ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِى وَلِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٥٠)
```

```english
And whencesoever you may go out, turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it, so that the people may have no allegation against you, neither those of them who are wrongdoers. So do not fear them, but fear Me, that I may complete My blessing on you and so that you may be guided. (150)
```

```arabic
كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيكُمْ رَسُولًا مِّنكُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا وَيُزَكِّيكُمْ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَيُعَلِّمُكُم مَّا لَمْ تَكُونُوا۟ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٥١)
```

```english
Even as We sent to you an apostle from among yourselves, who recites to you Our signs and purifies you, and teaches you the Book and wisdom, and teaches you what you did not know. (151)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are arranged in a coherent and orderly sequence within their context, as revealed by careful reflection upon them. They announce the designation of the Kaaba (الكعبة) as the direction of prayer (القبلة) — *the focal point toward which Muslims turn during worship* — for the Muslims. Therefore, no heed should be paid to the claim of those who say there is an advancement or delay in their order, or that they contain abrogating (الناسخ) and abrogated (المنسوخ) verses. They may narrate certain reports in this regard, but none of them are to be considered once they contradict the apparent meaning of the verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** "The foolish among the people will say, 'What has turned them away from the qiblah they were on?'" This serves as a second preamble to what Allah, the Exalted, will command regarding the adoption of the Kaaba as the direction of prayer (القبلة). It also provides instruction on how to answer the objections that would be raised by the foolish among the people. These were the Jews, out of prejudice for their own direction of prayer, which was the Holy House (بيت المقدس) — *Jerusalem* — and the polytheists among the Arabs, who were lying in wait for any new matter that might allow for dispute and contention. Allah had already prepared the ground for this first by mentioning the stories of Abraham (عليه السلام) and the various honors he received from Allah, the Glorified, as well as the honor of his son Ishmael (عليه السلام), their prayer for the Kaaba and Mecca, for the Prophet and the Muslim community, their building of the House, and the command to purify it for worship (العبادة).

It is well known that the change of the direction of prayer (القبلة) from the Holy House (بيت المقدس) to the Kaaba (الكعبة) was one of the greatest religious events and most significant legislations (التشريعات) encountered by the people after the Prophet's migration to Medina, as Islam began to establish its principles, disseminate its knowledge, and spread its truths. The Jews and others were not going to remain silent or at ease in the face of this legislation, for they saw it as nullifying one of their greatest religious boasts: the direction of prayer and the fact that others followed them in it, giving them a sense of superiority over those beneath them in this religious emblem. Furthermore, this was a brilliant advancement in the religion of the Muslims, as it unified their faces in their worship and religious rites toward a single point, freeing them from the outward divergence of faces and the inward fragmentation of word. Turning toward the Kaaba had a deeper impact and was more powerful in the souls of the Muslims—in the eyes of the Jews and the Arab polytheists, and especially the Jews—than matters like ritual purity (الطهارة) or supplication (الدعاء), as evidenced by their stories recounted in the Quran. They were a people who saw no reality (أصالة) in anything beyond the sensible world of nature, nor any weight in anything non-sensory. If a spiritual (معنوي) ruling from the rulings of Allah came to them, they accepted it without dispute; but if a formal (صوري) command from their Lord related to the sensible world of nature came to them—such as fighting, migration, prostration, or humility in speech—they met it with denial and resisted it with the utmost opposition.

In short, Allah, the Glorified, foretold the objections they would raise against the change of the direction of prayer and taught His Messenger how they should be answered to silence their claims.

As for their objection: it was that turning away from a direction of prayer that Allah, the Glorified, had legislated for His past prophets toward a house that possessed none of this inherent nobility—what is its justification? If it was by Allah's command, then Allah is the one who made the Holy House (بيت المقدس) the direction of prayer, so how could His ruling be overturned and His legislation abrogated? The Jews did not believe in abrogation (النسخ) — *the replacement of one divine ruling with another* — (as previously discussed in the verse of abrogation). And if it was without Allah's command, then it would be a deviation from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) and a departure from guidance (الهدى) into misguidance (الضلال). Although He did not explicitly mention this objection in His speech, the answer He provided alludes to it.

As for the answer: it is that designating a particular house, like the Kaaba, or a structure or physical body, like the Holy House or the stone within it, as the direction of prayer (القبلة) is not due to an inherent requirement (اقتضاء ذاتي) of that object such that it would be impossible to bypass it or fail to respond to its requirement—to the extent that the Holy House, in its status as the direction of prayer, would have an unchangeable ruling that could never be annulled. Rather, all physical bodies, structures, and all directions toward which a human being can turn are equal in that they do not inherently necessitate a ruling nor demand a legislation. All of them belong to Allah; He rules concerning them as He wills, how He wills, and when He wills. Whatever ruling He decrees is for the guidance of people according to what He intends for their individual and collective welfare and perfection. He does not decree except to guide thereby, and He does not guide except to that which is a straight path (صراط مستقيم) toward the perfection and welfare of the people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سيقول السفهاء من الناس** — **The foolish among the people will say.** He intended by them the Jews and the polytheists among the Arabs. That is why He referred to them as "the people" (الناس). He characterized them as foolish (سفهاء) due to the lack of rectitude in their primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and the deficiency of their judgment regarding the matter of legislation. Foolishness (السفاهة) is the lack of intellectual rectitude and the instability of judgment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما وليهم** — **What has turned them.** Turning (تولية) toward a thing or place means making it in front of the face, like facing (الاستقبال). The Exalted said: **فلنولينك قبلة ترضيها** — "We will surely turn you to a qiblah that pleases you." Turning away (التولية عن الشيء) means turning the face away from it, like turning one's back. The meaning is: what has diverted them or turned their faces away from the direction of prayer (القبلة) they were on, which was the Holy House (بيت المقدس), toward which the Prophet and the Muslims used to pray during his stay in Mecca and for several months after his migration to Medina. The direction of prayer was attributed to the Muslims even though the Jews were more ancient in praying toward it, in order to make the cause for wonder more striking and the objection more pointed. It was said, "What has turned *them* from *their* qiblah," rather than "What has turned the Prophet and the Muslims," for the reason we mentioned. Had it been said, "What has turned the Prophet and the Muslims from the qiblah of the Jews," the wonder would not have been as misplaced, and the answer would have been obvious to every listener with the slightest reflection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل لله المشرق والمغرب** — **Say, ‘To Allah belong the east and the west.’** He restricted Himself to these two among all directions because they define all other primary and secondary directions, such as north and south and what lies between the four cardinal points. The east (المشرق) and the west (المغرب) are relative directions determined by the rising and setting of the sun or stars. they encompass all points on earth except for two conceptual points, which are the true North and South poles. This is perhaps the reason for using "the east and the west" in place of all directions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يهدي من يشاء إلى صراط مستقيم** — **He guides whomever He wishes to a straight path.** The word "path" (صراط) is indefinite because the path varies according to the differences among nations in their readiness for guidance toward perfection and felicity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وكذلك جعلناكم امة وسطاً لتكونوا شهداء على الناس ويكون الرسول عليكم شهيداً** — **Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you.** The apparent meaning is: just as We shall turn the direction of prayer for you to guide you to a straight path, so too have We made you a middle nation (امة وسطاً). It has also been said that the meaning is: "And like this wondrous making, We have made you a middle nation" (though this is as you see it). As for the meaning of them being a "middle nation" and "witnesses to the people," the "middle" (الوسط) is that which lies between two extremes, leaning toward neither this side nor that. This nation, in relation to other people—namely the People of the Book and the polytheists—possesses this description. Some of them—the polytheists and idolaters—lean toward strengthening the physical side exclusively; they desire nothing but the life of this world and seeking perfection through its pleasures, finery, and ornaments. They hope for neither resurrection (بعث) nor rising (نشور), and they pay no heed to spiritual or moral virtues. Others, like the Christians, lean toward strengthening the spiritual side; they call for nothing but monasticism and the rejection of physical perfections that Allah, the Exalted, manifested in the forms of this material realm to be a complete means for attaining that for which man was created. These proponents of the spirit nullified the end by nullifying its cause, while those proponents of the body nullified the end by stopping at its cause and becoming rigid upon it. However, Allah, the Glorified, made this nation "middle" (وسطاً) by giving them a religion that guides its adherents to the even way between the two extremes, leaning toward neither these nor those. Rather, it strengthens both sides—the physical and the spiritual—according to what is appropriate for each, and it calls for the combination of both virtues. For man is a composite of spirit and body, neither pure spirit nor pure body. In his felicitous life, he needs to combine both perfections and both material and spiritual felicities. Thus, this nation is the "just middle" (الوسط العدل) by which both extremes of excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط) are measured and weighed. It is the witness (الشهيدة) over all other people situated at the extremes, and the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم)—who is the most perfect example of this nation—is a witness over the nation itself. Thus, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) is the scale by which the state of the individuals of the nation is weighed, and the nation is the scale by which the state of the people is weighed, and the reference point to which the extremes of excess and deficiency return.

This is what some commentators have established regarding the meaning of the verse, and it is in itself a correct meaning that is not without subtlety. However, it does not align with the wording of the verse. For the nation being "middle" (وسطاً) justifies its being a reference point to which the two extremes return and a scale by which the two sides are weighed, but not its being a "witness" (شاهدة) that testifies over the two sides or observes them. There is no clear correspondence between "middleness" in that sense and "witnessing" (الشهادة). Furthermore, there would then be no reason to mention the Messenger being a witness over the nation, as the Messenger's witnessing over the nation does not follow from the nation being made "middle" in the way a goal follows from its objective or a purpose from its subject.

Moreover, this witnessing (الشهادة) mentioned in the verse is one of the Quranic truths repeatedly mentioned in the speech of the Glorified. What appears from the instances of its mention is a meaning other than this. The Exalted said: **فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَٰؤُلَاءِ شَهِيدًا** — "So how shall it be, when We bring from every nation a witness and We bring you as a witness to them?" (4:41). And the Exalted said: **وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا ثُمَّ لَا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ** — "The day We shall raise a witness from every nation, then the faithless will not be permitted [to speak], nor will they be asked to satisfy [Allah]." (16:84). And: **وَوُضِعَ الْكِتَابُ وَجِيءَ بِالنَّبِيِّينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ** — "The Book will be set up, and the prophets and the witnesses will be brought..." (39:69). The witnessing in these verses is absolute, and the apparent meaning of all of them in their absoluteness is witnessing over the deeds of the nations, and also over the conveyance of the messengers, as hinted at by His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَنَسْأَلَنَّ الَّذِينَ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَنَسْأَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** — "We will surely question those to whom [Our messengers] were sent, and We will surely question the messengers." (7:6). Although this witnessing occurs in the Hereafter on the Day of Resurrection, its bearing (تحمل) occurs in this world, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted—narrating from Jesus (عليه السلام): **وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنتَ أَنتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ** — "I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had me taken up, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness to all things." (5:117). And the saying of the Exalted: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا** — "And on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them." (4:159).

It is clear that these ordinary senses within us, and the powers related to them, can only bear the forms of actions and deeds. Even that bearing only occurs for something that is present and perceived by the senses, not something non-existent or absent from them. As for the realities of deeds and the psychological meanings such as disbelief (الكفر), faith (الإيمان), success (الفوز), and loss (الخسران)—in short, everything hidden from the senses and internalized within the human being—these are what the hearts earn (تكسب القلوب), and upon them the reckoning of the Lord of the worlds revolves on the day when secrets are tested, as the Exalted said: **وَلَٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ** — "But He will take you to task for what your hearts have earned." (2:225). These are things that it is not within the power of a human being to enumerate, encompass, or distinguish among those present, let alone those absent—except for a man whose affairs Allah takes charge of and to whom He reveals that by His hand. This can be inferred from the saying of the Exalted: **وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** — "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who witness to the truth and who know [it]." (43:86). Jesus is certainly included in the exception in this verse—and Allah has testified that he is among the witnesses—as seen in the two previous verses. Thus, he is a witness to the truth and a knower of the reality.

In conclusion, this witnessing (الشهادة) is not the nation being upon a religion that combines physical and spiritual perfection, for that—besides not being the meaning of witnessing—is contrary to the apparent meaning of the noble verses. Rather, it is the bearing of the realities of people's deeds in this world—whether felicity or wretchedness, rejection or acceptance, obedience or rebellion—and the rendering of that in the Hereafter on the day when Allah calls for witnesses from everything, even from the human limbs, the day when the Messenger says: "O Lord, indeed my people took this Quran as something to be abandoned."

It is well known that this honor is not attained by the entire nation, as it is a special honor for the purified friends (الأولياء) among them. As for those below them among the intermediate in felicity and the just among the people of faith, they do not possess this, let alone the coarse and dry, or the tyrannical pharaohs of the nation. You will come to know in the saying of the Exalted: **وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولَٰئِكَ رَفِيقًا** — "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the witnesses and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!" (4:69), that the minimum description of the witnesses (الشهداء)—who are the witnesses of deeds—is that they are under the guardianship (ولاية) and blessing of Allah and are the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), as was briefly mentioned in the saying of the Exalted: **صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "The path of those whom You have blessed" (1:7).

Thus, the meaning of the nation being a witness is that this witnessing exists *among* them, just as the meaning of the Children of Israel being preferred over all the worlds is that this preference existed *among* them, without every single one of them being characterized by it. Rather, the description of the few was attributed to the whole because the few were part of it and from it. So, the nation being a witness means that among them are those who witness over the people, and the Messenger witnesses over them.

**If you say:** The saying of the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَالشُّهَدَاءُ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ** — "Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses with their Lord" (57:19), indicates that the general body of believers are witnesses.

**I say:** His saying, "with their Lord" (عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ), indicates that He will join them with the witnesses on the Day of Resurrection, though they did not attain it in this world. Similar to this is the saying of the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَانٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ** — "As for those who have faith and whose descendants followed them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them" (52:21). Furthermore, the verse is absolute, indicating that all believers from all nations are witnesses with Allah, without being exclusive to this nation, so it does not serve the objector's purpose.

**If you say:** Making this nation a "middle nation" in this sense does not entail that they, or some of them, are witnesses over deeds, nor that the Messenger is a witness over these witnesses. Thus, the objection applies to this explanation just as it applied to the previous one.

**I say:** The meaning of witnessing (الشهادة) is a goal (غاية) branching out in the verse from making the nation "middle" (وسطاً). Therefore, "middleness" must be a meaning that entails witnessing and witnesses. Allah, the Exalted, has said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ارْكَعُوا وَاسْجُدُوا وَاعْبُدُوا رَبَّكُمْ وَافْعَلُوا الْخَيْرَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ وَجَاهِدُوا فِي اللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهَادِهِ هُوَ اجْتَبَاكُمْ وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ مِّلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ مِن قَبْلُ وَفِي هَٰذَا لِيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِاللَّهِ هُوَ مَوْلَاكُمْ ، فَنِعْمَ الْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ** — "O you who have faith! Bow down and prostrate yourselves, and worship your Lord, and do good, so that you may be felicitous. And wage a jihad for the sake of Allah, a jihad which is worthy of Him. He has chosen you and has not placed for you any obstacle in religion, the faith of your father, Abraham. He named you ‘Muslims’ before, and in this, so that the Apostle may be a witness to you, and that you may be witnesses to mankind. So maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and hold fast to Allah. He is your master—an excellent master and an excellent helper." (22:77-78).

The Exalted made the Messenger being a witness over them and them being witnesses over the people a goal branching out from the choosing (الاجتباء) and the removal of hardship (الحرج) from them in religion. Then He defined the religion as the creed (الملة) that belonged to your father Abraham, who named you "Muslims" (المسلمين) before—that was when he prayed for you to his Lord and said: **وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ** — "and from our descendants a nation submissive to You." So Allah answered his prayer and made you Muslims, submitting (تسلمون) to His judgment and command without disobedience or disdain. For this reason, hardship was removed from you in religion; nothing in it is difficult or burdensome for you. Thus, you are the chosen ones (المجتبون), guided to the path, submitting to their Lord's judgment and command. We have made you so that the Messenger may be a witness over you and you may be witnesses over the people—that is, you stand in the middle between the Messenger and the people, connecting to them from one side. At that point, the reality of his (عليه السلام) prayer is realized in you and in the Messenger, where he said: **رَبَّنَا وَابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ** — "Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them" (2:129). Thus, you are a Muslim nation (أمة مسلمة) in whose hearts the Messenger has deposited the knowledge of the Book and wisdom, and you are purified by his purification. Purification (التزكية) is the cleansing from the impurities of the hearts and their devotion to servitude (العبودية), which is the meaning of Islam (الإسلام) as previously explained. Thus, you are Muslims, sincere in your servitude, and the Messenger has the first step, guidance, and upbringing in that. He has precedence over all, and you have the middle position by following him, while the rest of the people are on the other side. At the beginning and end of the verse, there are clues indicating the meaning we have derived from it, which are not hidden from those who reflect upon it; we shall explain them in their proper place, God willing.

It has become clear from what we have presented: **First**, that the nation being "middle" (وسطاً) entails both goals, and that His saying, the Exalted: "that you may be witnesses to the people, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you," are both necessary consequences of them being middle.

**Second:** That the nation being "middle" is by its being situated between the Messenger and the people, not by its being situated between the two extremes of excess and deficiency, or the sides of strengthening the spirit and strengthening the body among people.

**Third:** That the verse, in terms of meaning, is linked to the verses of Abraham's (عليه السلام) prayer, and that witnessing (الشهادة) is among the affairs of the Muslim nation.

Know that witnessing over deeds, according to what His speech, the Exalted, provides, is not restricted to the witnesses among human beings. Rather, everything that has some connection to the deed—such as the angels, time, place, religion, the Book, the limbs, the senses, and the heart—has a witnessing in it. It is inferred from this that what is present on the Day of Resurrection is that which was in this worldly realm, and that these things have a kind of conscious life (حيوة شاعرة) by which they bear the specifics of deeds, and these deeds are imprinted upon them. It is not necessary that the life in everything be of the same type as the life of the animal genus, possessing properties and effects like its properties and effects, such that necessity would reject it. There is no evidence to restrict the types of life to a single type. This is a summary of the matter in this context; as for the detailed discussion of each one individually, it is deferred to its appropriate place.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وما جعلنا القبلة التي كنت عليها إلا لنعلم من يتبع الرسول ممن ينقلب على عقبيه** — **We did not appoint the qiblah you were on except that We may know those who follow the Apostle from those who turn back on their heels.** The meaning of His saying, "that We may know" (لنعلم), is either the knowledge of the messengers and prophets, for example, because great ones speak on behalf of themselves and their followers—like a commander saying, "We killed so-and-so and imprisoned so-and-so," when it was his followers who did it. Or it refers to the actual, existential knowledge (العلم العيني الفعلي) from Him, the Exalted, which occurs with creation and origination, rather than the knowledge prior to origination.

"Turning back on the heels" (الانقلاب على العقبين) is a metaphor for turning away (الإعراض). For a person—standing on his heels—if he turns from one direction to another, he turns on his heels. Thus, it was made a metaphor for turning away, similar to His saying: **وَمَن يُوَلِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ دُبُرَهُ** — "and whoever turns his back to them on that day" (8:16). The apparent meaning of the verse is that it is a rebuttal to what might occur in the hearts of the believers regarding the change of the direction of prayer and its abrogation, and regarding the status of the prayers they had performed toward the previous direction.

From this, it appears that the "qiblah you were on" refers to the Holy House (بيت المقدس) rather than the Kaaba. There is no evidence for the Holy House being made the direction of prayer twice, nor the Kaaba being made so twice; for if the "qiblah" in the verse meant the Kaaba, it would necessitate what was mentioned.

In short, it was expected that it might occur in the hearts of the believers: First, since it was destined that the direction of prayer would ultimately settle on the Kaaba, what was the reason, initially, for making the Holy House the direction of prayer? So the Glorified explained that these rulings and legislations are only for the sake of interests related to the upbringing and perfection of people, the testing of the believers from others, and the distinguishing of the obedient from the disobedient, and the submissive from the rebellious. The reason necessitating the previous direction of prayer in your case is this very same reason. Thus, the meaning of His saying, "except that We may know those who follow the Apostle," is: except to distinguish those who follow you. The shift from the second-person address to the third-person ("the Apostle") is due to the relevance of the attribute of messengership in this distinction. The "appointing of the previous qiblah" refers to its appointment for the Muslims. If the original appointment of the Holy House as a qiblah was intended, then it refers to the Messenger in an absolute sense, and the speech would be about His messengers without specific regard, though this is somewhat distant from the context.

**Second:** What is the state of the prayers the Muslims had performed toward the Holy House, given that they were performed toward something other than the [current] direction of prayer? The answer is: the direction of prayer remains the direction of prayer as long as it is not abrogated. When Allah, the Glorified, abrogates a ruling, He removes it from the time of abrogation, not from its origin, out of His kindness and mercy toward the believers. This is what He pointed to by His saying: **وما كان الله ليضيع أعمالكم ، ان الله بالناس لرؤف رحيم** — "Allah would not let your prayers go to waste. Indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to mankind." The difference between kindness (الرأفة) and mercy (الرحمة), while they share the same root meaning, is that kindness is specific to the one in distress and need, while mercy is more general.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قد نرى تقلب وجهك في السماء فلنولينك قبلة ترضيها** — **We certainly see you turning your face about in the sky. We will surely turn you to a qiblah that pleases you.** The verse indicates that before the revelation of the verse of the qiblah—which is this verse—the Messenger of Allah used to turn his face toward the horizons of the sky, and that this was out of anticipation or expectation for the revelation of revelation (الوحي) regarding the matter of the direction of prayer. This was because he wished for Allah, the Exalted, to honor him with a direction of prayer specific to him, not because he was dissatisfied with the Holy House as a direction of prayer—far be it from the Messenger of Allah to feel so. As the Exalted said: "We will surely turn you to a qiblah that pleases you," for being pleased with something does not necessitate being displeased with its alternative. Rather, the Jews—according to the narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verse—used to taunt the Muslims for following their direction of prayer and would boast over them because of it. This saddened the Messenger of Allah, so he went out in the darkness of the night, turning his face toward the sky, waiting for revelation from Allah, the Glorified, to relieve his distress. So the verse was revealed. Had it been revealed to remain with the previous direction, it would have been an argument for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) against the Jews. It was not a shame for the Messenger of Allah or the Muslims to face their direction of prayer, for a servant has nothing but obedience and acceptance. However, it was revealed with a new direction of prayer, thus cutting off their taunting and boasting, in addition to defining the obligation. So it was both an argument and a source of pleasure.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فول وجهك شطر المسجد الحرام وحيث ما كنتم فولوا وجوهكم شطره** — **so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it.** The "side" (الشطر) means the part, and the side of the Holy Mosque (المسجد الحرام) is the Kaaba. In His saying, "the side of the Holy Mosque," rather than saying, "turn your face toward the Kaaba," or "turn your face toward the Holy House," there is a correspondence with the ruling of the previous direction of prayer, for it was toward the side of the Furthest Mosque (المسجد الأقصى)—which is the well-known Rock there. So it was replaced by the side of the Holy Mosque—which is the Kaaba. Furthermore, adding "side" to "the Mosque" and describing the Mosque as "Holy" (الحرام) provides advantages to the ruling that would be lost if it were said "the Kaaba" or "the Holy House."

The specific address to the Messenger of Allah first with the command "turn your face," followed by the generalization of the ruling to him and other believers with the command "and wherever you may be," supports the view that the direction of prayer was changed while the Messenger of Allah was standing in prayer in the mosque—with the Muslims with him. So the command was directed specifically to him first in his personal prayer, then followed by the general ruling encompassing him and others, for all times and places.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وان الذين اوتوا الكتاب ليعلمون انه الحق من ربهم** — **Indeed those who were given the Book know that it is the truth from their Lord.** This is because their Book contained the truth of the prophethood of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), or that the direction of prayer for this truthful Prophet would be toward the side of the Holy Mosque. In any case, His saying, "those who were given the Book," indicates that their Book contained the truth of this legislation, either explicitly or implicitly. "And Allah is not oblivious of what they do" in terms of concealing the truth and withholding the knowledge they possess.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ولئن أتيت الذين اوتوا الكتاب بكل آية** — **Even if you were to bring every sign to those who were given the Book.** This is a rebuke to them for their stubbornness and obstinacy, and that their refusal to accept is not due to the truth being hidden from them or not being clear to them. For they know it is the truth with a knowledge unmixed with doubt. Rather, the motive for them to spread objections and stir up fitna (الفتنة) — *discord and trial* — is their stubbornness in religion and their denial of the truth. Thus, no argument benefits them, and no sign cuts off their denial. So, even if you brought them every sign, they would not follow your direction of prayer due to their stubbornness and denial. "And you will not follow their qiblah," because you are upon a clear proof from your Lord. It is possible that His saying, "And you will not," is a prohibition in the form of a report. "Nor do some of them follow the qiblah of others"—these are the Jews who face the Rock of the Holy House wherever they are, and the Christians who face the East wherever they are. Neither this group accepts the direction of the other, nor does the other accept the direction of this one, out of following their desires.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ولئن اتبعت اهوائهم من بعد ما جائك من العلم** — **And if you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you.** This is a threat to the Prophet, but the meaning is directed toward his nation. It is an indication that in this rebellion, they are only following their desires and that they are thereby wrongdoers (ظالمون).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الذين آتيناهم الكتاب يعرفونه كما يعرفون أبنائهم** — **Those whom We have given the Book recognize him just as they recognize their own sons.** The pronoun in His saying, "recognize him" (يعرفونه), refers to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) rather than the Book. The evidence for this is the comparison of this recognition to the recognition of sons, for that is only appropriate for a human being. It is not said of a book that someone recognizes or knows it as he knows his son. Furthermore, the context of the speech—which is about the Messenger of Allah and what was revealed to him regarding the matter of the direction of prayer—is foreign to the subject of the Book given to the People of the Book. So the meaning is that the People of the Book recognize the Messenger of Allah through the glad tidings in their books just as they recognize their own sons. "And indeed a group of them knowingly conceal the truth."

Accordingly, there is a shift (التفات) in the speech from presence to absence in His saying, "recognize him." The Messenger of Allah is treated as absent, and the address is directed to the believers after he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) had been present and the address was with him. This is to clarify that his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) matter is clear and manifest to the People of the Book. Such a structure is like the speech of one who speaks to a group but singles out one of them for address to show his merit; he addresses him while others listen. Then, when he reaches the merit and honor specific to the person addressed, he turns from addressing him to addressing the group. Then, after finishing the explanation of his merit, he turns back to the original direction of the address to him. By this, the point of the shift (الالتفات) becomes clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الحق من ربك فلا تكونن من الممترين** — **[This is] the truth from your Lord; so do not be among the doubters.** This is a confirmation of the previous explanation and a stern prohibition against doubting (الامتراء) — *skepticism and uncertainty*. The apparent address is to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), but its meaning is for the nation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ولكل وجهة هو موليها فاستبقوا الخيرات** — **Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so take the lead in all good works.** The "direction" (الوجهة) is that toward which one turns, like the qiblah. This is a return to the summary of the previous explanation, replacing it with another explanation that guides people to stop pursuing the matter of the direction of prayer and talking excessively about it. The meaning is that every people has a legislated direction of prayer according to what their interests necessitate; it is not an inherent, existential ruling that does not accept change or transformation. So, it is not important for you to debate and quarrel over it. Leave that and "take the lead in all good works" and hasten toward them by competing, for Allah will gather you all to a day about which there is no doubt. "Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together; indeed Allah has power over all things."

Know that just as the verse is applicable to the matter of the direction of prayer due to its location among its verses, it is also applicable to the matter of creation (التكوين). It contains an indication of destiny (القدر) and decree (القضاء), and the establishment of rulings and etiquettes to realize them. The full explanation of this will come in its specific place, God willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ومن حيث خرجت فولّ وجهك شطر المسجد الحرام** — **Whencesoever you may go out, turn your face towards the Holy Mosque.** Some commentators mentioned that the meaning is: from whatever place you go out, and in whatever land you settle, turn your face. Others mentioned that the meaning is: from whichever of the lands you go out. It is possible that the meaning of His saying, "Whencesoever you may go out," is Mecca, from which the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) went out, as the Exalted said: **مِّن قَرْيَتِكَ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَتْكَ** — "from your town which expelled you" (47:13). The meaning would be that facing the House is a fixed ruling for you in Mecca and other lands and regions. In His saying, "and it is indeed the truth from your Lord, and Allah is not oblivious of what you do," there is confirmation and emphasis.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ومن حيث خرجت فولّ وجهك شطر المسجد الحرام وحيث ما كنتم فولُّوا وجوهكم شطره** — **Whencesoever you may go out, turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it.** The repetition of the first sentence with its exact wording is perhaps to indicate the stability of its ruling in every state. It is like someone saying: "Fear Allah when you stand, and fear Allah when you sit, and fear Allah when you speak, and fear Allah when you are silent," meaning: adhere to godwariness (التقوى) in each of these states and let it be with you. Had it been said, "Fear Allah when you stand, sit, speak, and are silent," this point would have been lost. The meaning is: face the side of the Holy Mosque from the place you went out, and wherever you may be on earth, turn your faces toward it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لئلا يكون للناس عليكم حجة إلا الذين ظلموا منهم فلا تخشوهم واخشوني** — **so that the people may have no argument against you, except those of them who are wrongdoers. So do not fear them, but fear Me.** This is an explanation of three benefits in this ruling, which contains the strongest emphasis on adhering to compliance and warning against opposition:

**One of them:** That the Jews knew from their books that the promised Prophet's direction of prayer would be the Kaaba rather than the Holy House, as the Exalted said: "Indeed those who were given the Book know that it is the truth from their Lord." Abandoning this ruling would give the Jews an argument against the Muslims that the Prophet is not the promised Prophet. But adhering to this ruling and acting upon it cuts off their argument, "except those of them who are wrongdoers" (إلا الذين ظلموا منهم)—this is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع), meaning: but those among them who are wrongdoers by following their desires will not cease because of that. So do not fear them, for they are wrongdoers following their desires, and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people; and fear Me.

**The second:** That adhering to this ruling leads the Muslims to the completion of the blessing (تمام النعمة) upon them through the perfection of their religion. We shall explain the meaning of the completion of the blessing in the discussion of the saying of the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي** — "Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you" (5:3).

**The third:** The hope of being guided to the straight path. The meaning of guidance (الاهتداء) has already been discussed in the commentary on the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ** — "Guide us on the straight path" (1:6).

Some commentators mentioned that the inclusion in this verse—the verse of the change of the direction of prayer—of the phrase "and that I may complete My blessing upon you and so that you may be guided," along with the inclusion of these two phrases in the saying of the Exalted in Surah al-Fath regarding the conquest of Mecca, where the Exalted said: **إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَاطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا** — "Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come, and that He may complete His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path" (48:1-2), indicates that it contains the glad tidings of the conquest of Mecca.

The explanation of this is that the Kaaba, at the beginning of Islam, was occupied by the idols and images of the polytheists, and the authority was with them. Islam had not yet become strong enough to manifest its dominance and power. So Allah guided His Messenger to face the Holy House, as it was the direction of prayer for the Jews, who were closer in their religion to Islam than the polytheists. Then, when the matter of Islam became manifest with the migration of the Messenger of Allah to Medina, and the time of the conquest drew near, and the purification of the House from the filth of idols was expected, the command came to change the direction of prayer. This was the great blessing (النعمة) that was specific to the Muslims. In the verse of the change, the completion of the blessing and guidance was promised, which is the clearing of the Kaaba from the impurities of idols and its designation as the direction of prayer toward which Allah is worshipped, with the Muslims being the ones specific to it and it being specific to them. Thus, it is a glad tiding of the conquest of Mecca. Then, when the conquest of Mecca was mentioned at the time it occurred, He pointed to what He had promised them of the completion of the blessing and the glad tiding by His saying: "and that He may complete His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path."

This discourse, although seemingly plausible, is devoid of deep reflection. The apparent meaning of the verses does not support it. For that which indicates the promise of the completion of the blessing in this verse: "and that I may complete My blessing upon you and so that you may be guided," is the "lam" of purpose (لام الغاية). The verse of Surah al-Fath, which he took as the fulfillment of this promise and the realization of this glad tiding—namely His saying, the Exalted: "that Allah may forgive you... and that He may complete His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path"—contains this very same "lam." Thus, both verses contain the beautiful promise of the completion of the blessing. Furthermore, the verse of the Hajj [or rather, the qiblah] contains the promise of the completion of the blessing for all Muslims, while the verse of al-Fath contains it for the Messenger of Allah specifically. Thus, the context in the two verses is different.

If there were a verse recounting the fulfillment of the promise contained in the two verses, it would be the saying of the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلَامَ دِينًا** — "Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I am pleased with Islam as your religion" (5:3). The discussion on the meaning of blessing (النعمة) and the identification of this blessing with which Allah, the Glorified, favors [the believers] in the verse will follow.

Similar to these two verses in containing the promise of the completion of the blessing is the saying of the Exalted: **وَلَٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** — "but He desires to purify you and to complete His blessing upon you so that you may be grateful" (5:6), and the saying of the Exalted: **كَذَٰلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ** — "Thus does He complete His blessing upon you so that you may submit" (16:81). Some discussion appropriate to this context will follow, God willing, in the commentary on these verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كما أرسلنا فيكم رسولاً منكم** — **As We sent to you an apostle from among yourselves.** The apparent meaning of the verse is that the "kaf" is for comparison and the "ma" is infinitival (مصدرية). So the meaning is: We have blessed you by making the House built by Abraham—for which he prayed for blessings and goodness—your direction of prayer, just as We sent among you a Messenger from among yourselves who recites Our signs to you, teaches you the Book and wisdom, and purifies you, in response to the prayer of Abraham, when he and his son Ishmael said: "Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them." In this, there is a favor (امتنان) upon them for the sending of the Messenger, just as there is a favor for making the Kaaba the direction of prayer. From here, it appears that the one addressed by His saying, "among you a Messenger from among yourselves," is the Muslim nation—specifically the guardians of the religion among the nation in reality, and all Muslims from the progeny of Ishmael (the Mudar Arabs) in appearance, and all Arabs, indeed all Muslims, according to the ruling.

His saying, the Exalted: **يتلوا عليكم آياتنا** (who recites to you Our signs): It is evident that these are the signs (آيات) of the Quran because of His saying "recites" (يتلو), for the primary concern in recitation (التلاوة) is directed toward the wording rather than the meaning. Purification (التزكية) is cleansing (التطهير), which is the removal of impurities and filth (الأدناس والقذارات); thus, it encompasses the removal of corrupt beliefs such as polytheism (الشرك) and disbelief (الكفر), the removal of base traits (الملكات الرذيلة) of character such as arrogance and stinginess, and the removal of heinous acts and deeds such as murder, adultery, and drinking wine. The teaching of the Book (الكتاب) and Wisdom (الحكمة), and the teaching of what they did not know, encompasses all fundamental (الأصلية) and derivative (الفرعية) branches of knowledge.

Know that these noble verses contain instances of grammatical shift (الالتفات) — *the transition between different grammatical persons to highlight specific meanings* — where He, the Exalted, is referred to in the third person, the first person singular, and the first person plural. Similarly, others besides Him, the Exalted, are referred to in the third person, the second person, and the first person. The subtle point (النكتة) behind this is not hidden from the insightful contemplator.

## (Narrative Discourse)

In *al-Majma‘*, citing al-Qummi in his *Tafsir* regarding His saying, the Exalted: **سَيَقُولُ السُّفَهَاءُ** (The foolish among the people will say...), narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "The direction of prayer (القبلة) was changed to the Kaaba (الكعبة) after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had prayed toward Jerusalem (بيت المقدس) for thirteen years in Mecca. After his migration to Medina, he prayed toward Jerusalem for seven months." He said: "Then Allah directed him toward Mecca. This was because the Jews used to taunt the Messenger of Allah, saying: 'You are a follower of ours, praying toward our *qiblah*.' The Messenger of Allah became deeply distressed by this. He went out in the middle of the night, looking toward the horizons of the sky, awaiting a command from Allah regarding this matter. When morning came and the time for the noon prayer (الظهر) arrived, he was in the Mosque of Bani Salim. He had already prayed two units (ركعتين) of the noon prayer when Gabriel descended, took him by his arms, and turned him toward the Kaaba, and He revealed to him: **قَدْ نَرَىٰ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِي السَّمَاءِ فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَاهَا فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ** (We certainly see you turning your face about in the sky. We will surely turn you to a qiblah of your liking: so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque [2:144]). Thus, he had prayed two units toward Jerusalem and two units toward the Kaaba. Then the Jews and the foolish ones said: 'What has turned them from the *qiblah* they used to face?'"

**I say:** The narrations transmitted through both Sunni and Shi'a chains are numerous, preserved in the compendiums of hadith, and are similar in content. There is disagreement regarding the date of this event; most reports—and this is the most correct view—state it occurred in Rajab of the second year of the Hijra, the seventeenth month thereof. Some matters related to this topic will be discussed in a separate section, God willing.

It is narrated through the chains of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah* regarding the testimony of this nation (الأمة) over the people, and the Prophet's testimony over them, that on the Day of Resurrection, the nations will deny that the prophets conveyed the message. Allah will then demand proof from the prophets that they had conveyed it—though He knows best—and the nation of Muhammad will be brought forward to testify. The other nations will ask: "How do you know?" They will reply: "We knew that through the report of Allah, the Exalted, in His speaking Book, upon the tongue of His truthful Prophet." Then Muhammad will be brought and asked about the state of his nation; he will attest to their purity (يزكيهم) and testify to their justice (عدالتهم). This is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ** (So how shall it be, when We bring from every nation a witness... [4:41]).

**I say:** What this report contains—which is supported by other reports transmitted by al-Suyuti in *al-Durr al-Manthur* and others—regarding the Messenger of Allah's attestation to the purity of his nation and his declaration of their justice (تعديله), perhaps refers to his declaration of justice for some of them rather than all of them. Otherwise, it is refuted by the established necessity (الضرورة) of the Book and the Sunnah. How could one validate or justify these atrocities, the likes of which can hardly be found, nor even a prototype of them, in any of the past nations? And how can the Pharaohs and tyrants (طواغيتها) of this nation be purified and declared just? Is this anything but an assault on the upright religion (الدين الحنيف) and a mockery of the realities of this luminous faith (الملة البيضاء)? Furthermore, the hadith involves the endorsement of theoretical testimony (الشهادة النظرية) rather than the testimony of direct witnessing (شهادة التحمل).

In *al-Manaqib*, regarding this meaning, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "None shall be witnesses (شهداء) over the people except the Imams and the Messengers. As for the nation (الأمة), it is not permissible for Allah to call them as witnesses when among them are those whose testimony would not be accepted for a bundle of herbs."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لِّتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيدًا** (...that you may be witnesses to mankind, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you [2:143]): "If you think that Allah meant by this verse all the people of the *qiblah* from among the monotheists (الموحدين), do you then see that one whose testimony is not accepted in this world for a measure of dates will have his testimony sought by Allah on the Day of Resurrection and accepted from him in the presence of all the past nations? Never! Allah did not mean such as these from His creation. He meant the nation for whom the prayer of Abraham was answered: **كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ** (You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind [3:110]); they are the middle nation (الأمة الوسطى) and they are the best nation brought forth for mankind."

**I say:** The explanation of this has already passed in the commentary on the verse, derived from the Book.

In *Qurb al-Isnad*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), from his father, from the Prophet, who said: "Among what Allah gave my nation and favored them with over other nations are three characteristics He did not give except to a prophet..."—until he said—"And when He sent a prophet, He made him a witness over his people, and indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, made my nation a witness over creation, where He says: **لِيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ** (...that the Apostle may be a witness to you and that you may be witnesses to mankind [22:78])."

**I say:** This hadith does not contradict what has passed, for the "nation" intended is the submitted nation (الأمة المسلمة) for whom the prayer of Abraham was answered.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a hadith describing the Day of Resurrection, he (peace be upon him) said: "They will gather in a station where all of creation will be made to speak, and no one shall speak except him whom the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) permits and who speaks aright. Then the Messenger will be stood up and questioned; that is His saying to Muhammad: **فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جِئْنَا مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ بِشَهِيدٍ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ عَلَىٰ هَٰؤُلَاءِ شَهِيدًا** (So how shall it be, when We bring from every nation a witness and We bring you as a witness against them? [4:41]). He is the witness over the witnesses, and the witnesses are the Messengers."

In *al-Tahdhib*, narrated from Abu Basir from one of the two Imams (peace be upon them), he said: "I said to him: 'Did He command him to pray toward Jerusalem?' He said: 'Yes. Do you not see that Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, says: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الْقِبْلَةَ الَّتِي كُنتَ عَلَيْهَا إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يَتَّبِعُ الرَّسُولَ مِمَّن يَنقَلِبُ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ** (We did not appoint the qiblah you were following except that We may distinguish those who follow the Apostle from those who turn back on their heels [2:143])?'"

**I say:** The implication of the hadith is that His saying, the Exalted, "which you were following" (التي كنت عليها) is a description of the *qiblah*, meaning Jerusalem, and that it was the *qiblah* the Messenger of Allah was following. This is supported by the context of the verses as previously mentioned.

From here, support is found for what is in some reports from Imam al-Askari (peace be upon him): "The desire of the people of Mecca was for the Kaaba, so Allah wanted to distinguish the follower of Muhammad from his opponents by following the *qiblah* they disliked while Muhammad commanded it. And since the desire of the people of Medina was for Jerusalem, He commanded them to oppose it and turn toward the Kaaba to distinguish who follows Muhammad in what he dislikes, thus being his confirmer and in agreement with him." This also clarifies the invalidity of what has been said: that His saying, the Exalted, "which you were following" (التي كنت عليها) is the second object of "We appointed" (جعلنا), meaning: "We did not appoint the *qiblah*—meaning the Kaaba—which you were following before Jerusalem..." They argued for this using His saying: "except that We may distinguish those who follow the Apostle." This is invalid, and its invalidity is clear from what has preceded.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from al-Zubayri from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'Will you not inform me about faith (الإيمان)? Is it action and speech, or speech without action?' He said: 'Faith is entirely action, and speech is part of that action, an obligation from Allah, clarified in His Book, its light is clear and its proof is firm; the Book testifies to it and calls toward it. When Allah turned His Prophet toward the Kaaba away from Jerusalem, the Muslims said to the Prophet: "What do you see regarding our prayers that we used to pray toward Jerusalem? What is our state regarding them, and what is the state of those of our dead who passed away while they were praying toward Jerusalem?" So Allah revealed: **وَمَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَانَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِالنَّاسِ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ** (...and Allah would not let your faith go to waste. Indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to mankind [2:143]). Thus, He named the prayer "faith." So whoever is wary of Allah, guarding his limbs and fulfilling for every one of his limbs what Allah has obligated upon it, will meet Allah with his faith complete, being among the people of Paradise. And whoever betrays any part of it or transgresses what Allah commanded regarding it, will meet Allah with deficient faith.'"

**I say:** Al-Kulayni also narrated it. Its inclusion of the revelation of His saying: "and Allah would not let your faith go to waste" after the change of the *qiblah* does not contradict the previous explanation.

In *al-Faqih*, it is mentioned that the Prophet prayed toward Jerusalem for thirteen years in Mecca and nineteen months in Medina. Then the Jews taunted him, saying: "You are a follower of our *qiblah*." He became deeply distressed by this. During part of the night, he went out turning his face toward the horizons of the sky. When morning came, he prayed the dawn prayer (الغداة). When he had prayed two units of the noon prayer, Gabriel came and said to him: **قَدْ نَرَىٰ تَقَلُّبَ وَجْهِكَ فِي السَّمَاءِ فَلَنُوَلِّيَنَّكَ قِبْلَةً تَرْضَاهَا فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ** (We certainly see you turning your face about in the sky. We will surely turn you to a qiblah of your liking: so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque [2:144]). Then he took the Prophet's hand and turned his face toward the Kaaba, and those behind him turned their faces until the men stood in the place of the women and the women in the place of the men. Thus, the first part of his prayer was toward Jerusalem and the last part toward the Kaaba. The news reached a mosque in Medina while its people had prayed two units of the afternoon prayer (العصر), so they turned toward the *qiblah*. The first part of their prayer was toward Jerusalem and the last part toward the Kaaba, so that mosque was named the Mosque of the Two Qiblahs (مسجد القبلتين).

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated something similar, and that the Prophet was in the Mosque of Bani Salim.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ** (...so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque [2:144]), he said: "Face the *qiblah*, and do not turn your face away from the *qiblah*, for it would invalidate your prayer. Indeed, Allah says to His Prophet regarding the obligatory prayer (الفريضة): 'so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it.'"

**I say:** The reports stating that the verse was revealed regarding the obligatory prayer and is specific to it are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ** (Those whom We have given the Book recognize him... [2:146]), he said: "This verse was revealed regarding the Jews and the Christians. Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, says: 'And those whom We have given the Book recognize him,' meaning they recognize the Messenger of Allah as they recognize their own sons, because Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, had revealed to them in the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms the description of Muhammad, the description of his companions, and his migration. This is His saying, the Exalted: **مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ اللَّهِ ۚ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَاءُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ تَرَاهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانًا ۖ سِيمَاهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِم مِّنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْرَاةِ ۚ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنجِيلِ** (Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure. Their mark is on their faces from the effect of prostration. That is their description in the Torah, and their description in the Evangel... [48:29]). This is the description of the Messenger of Allah in the Torah and the description of his companions. When Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, sent him, the People of the Book recognized him, just as He, Majestic is His Majesty, said: **فَلَمَّا جَاءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ** (...so when there came to them that which they recognized, they denied it [2:89])."

**I say:** Something similar was narrated in *al-Kafi* from Ali (peace be upon him).

In numerous reports from Shi'a chains, His saying, the Exalted: **أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا يَأْتِ بِكُمُ اللَّهُ جَمِيعًا** (...Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together [2:148]) refers to the companions of the Upholder (القائم) — *the promised savior from the progeny of the Prophet*. In some of them, it is stated that this is by way of application (التطبيق) and flow (الجري) — *the principle that Quranic verses apply to new instances over time*.

In a hadith from Sunni chains regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِي عَلَيْكُمْ** (...and that I may complete My blessing upon you [2:150]), narrated from Ali: "The completion of the blessing is dying upon Islam."

In another hadith from their chains: "The completion of the blessing is entering Paradise."

## (Scientific Discourse)

The legislation of the *qiblah* in Islam and the requirement of facing it in prayer—which is a universal worship among Muslims—as well as in slaughtering animals and other matters that concern the general public, made people most in need of researching the direction of the *qiblah* and determining it. Initially, this was done through conjecture, estimation, and a type of guesswork. Later, the general need prompted mathematicians among their scholars to bring it closer to precision. They utilized the tables set in astronomical handbooks (الزيجات) to determine the latitude (عرض) and longitude (طول) of cities. They derived the deviation (انحراف) of Mecca from the southern point in a city—that is, the deviation of the line connecting the city and Mecca from the line connecting the city and the southern point (the meridian line/خط نصف النهار)—using sines (الجيوب) and trigonometry (المثلثات). Then they determined this for every Muslim city using the well-known Indian Circle (الدائرة الهندية) which determines the meridian line, followed by the degrees of deviation and the line of the *qiblah*.

Later, to speed up the process and for ease of use, they employed the magnetic instrument known as the compass (الحك). With its needle, it determines the directions of North and South, thus substituting for the Indian Circle in determining the southern point. By knowing the degree of deviation of the city, the user can identify the direction of the *qiblah*.

However, this effort of theirs—may Allah reward their efforts—was not free from deficiency and error from two perspectives. As for the first: later mathematicians discovered that the predecessors had erred in determining longitude, which affected the calculation of deviation and thus the identification of the direction of the Kaaba. This was because their method for determining latitude—which is recording the elevation of the North Pole—was closer to precision, unlike the method for determining longitude, which was recording the distance between two points sharing a common celestial event like an eclipse by the amount of the sun's apparent movement, which is estimation by the hour. This was difficult and lacked precision with ancient tools. However, the availability of modern tools and the closeness of communications today has made the matter entirely easy. Yet the need remained until the eminent and famous scholar, al-Sardar al-Kabuli—may Allah have mercy on him—undertook this task in recent times. He derived the *qiblah* deviation using modern principles and wrote his well-known treatise *Tuhfat al-Ajillah fi Ma‘rifat al-Qiblah*. It is an elegant treatise in which he explained the method of deriving the *qiblah* through mathematical demonstration and provided tables for determining the *qiblah* of various cities.

Among the most beautiful things he was granted success in—may Allah reward his effort—is what he revealed of a brilliant miracle (كرامة) for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his preserved prayer niche (المحراب) in the Prophet's Mosque in Medina. This is because Medina, according to the calculations of the ancients, was at latitude 25 degrees and longitude 75 degrees 20 minutes. The *qiblah* of the Prophet's prayer niche in his mosque did not align with this. Therefore, scholars were constantly researching the matter of the prayer niche's *qiblah*, sometimes mentioning reasons for its deviation that did not correspond to the reality of the matter. However, he—may Allah have mercy on him—clarified that Medina is at latitude 24 degrees 57 minutes and longitude 39 degrees 59 minutes, with a deviation of approximately 0 degrees 45 minutes. The *qiblah* of the prayer niche aligned with this perfectly. Thus appeared a brilliant miracle for the Prophet in his *qiblah* toward which he turned his face while in prayer, and it was mentioned that Gabriel took his hand and turned his face toward the Kaaba. Allah and His Messenger have spoken the truth.

After him, the eminent engineer and leader Abdul Razzaq al-Bagha'iri—may Allah have mercy on him—derived the *qiblah* for most regions of the earth and published a treatise on knowing the *qiblah*, which are tables mentioning one thousand five hundred locations on earth. With this, the blessing in identifying the *qiblah* was completed.

As for the second perspective, which is the magnetic perspective: they found that the magnetic poles of the Earth do not coincide with its geographical poles. The magnetic North Pole, for instance—noting that it changes over time—is approximately one thousand miles away from the geographical North Pole. Based on this, the compass does not identify the geographical South Pole itself; rather, the discrepancy might reach an amount that cannot be overlooked. This prompted the eminent mathematical engineer and leader Husayn Ali Razm Ara in these days—the year 1332 Solar Hijri—to solve this dilemma. He derived the amount of discrepancy between the geographical and magnetic poles according to different points and identified the *qiblah* deviation from the magnetic pole for approximately one thousand locations on earth. He invented a compass that provides an approximation very close to precision in identifying the *qiblah*, and it is in use today—may Allah reward his effort.

## (Social Discourse)

One who contemplates the affairs of human society and looks at the properties and effects that follow this matter called "society" (الاجتماع)—from the perspective that it is a society—will not doubt that this society was formed, then branched out and expanded into its various divisions and facets, by human nature (الطبيعة الانسانية). This occurred when it sensed, through inspiration from Allah, the Glorified, its needs for survival and perfection through social acts. Thus, it resorts to society and adheres to it to succeed in its actions, movements, and stillness within the cradle of social upbringing and with its assistance. Then it sensed and was inspired with knowledge (mental images/صور ذهنية) and perceptions that it applies to matter, to its needs therein, and to its actions and the directions of its actions. These serve as the link and connection between it and its actions and needs, such as the belief in beauty and ugliness (الحسن والقبح), what is obligatory, what is appropriate, and other social principles like leadership and followership, ownership and exclusivity, common and specific transactions, and other general rules, laws, customs, and national traditions that are not free from change and variation according to different peoples, regions, and eras. All these meanings and the rules established upon them are the product of human nature through inspiration from Allah, the Glorified. Human nature refined them to represent the meanings it believes in and desires in the external world, then moves toward them through action, deed, omission, and perfection.

The devotional turning (التوجه العبادي) toward Allah, the Glorified—Who is transcendent above the affairs of matter and sanctified beyond the attachment of material sense—if it is intended to transcend the limits of the heart and conscience and descend into the realm of actions (which only revolve around material things), there is no alternative or escape but for it to be by way of representation (التمثيل). This is done by observing the heart's turnings in their various specificities, then representing them in action through suitable forms and shapes of deeds. For example, prostration (السجدة) is intended for self-abasement, bowing (الركوع) for glorification, circumambulation (الطواف) for the sacrifice of the self, standing (القيام) for magnifying, and ablution (الوضوء) and bathing (الغسل) for purity for the Presence, and so on. There is no doubt that turning toward the Worshipped and facing Him by the servant in his servitude is the spirit of his worship, without which it would have no life or existence. To represent this, worship needs it for its perfection, stability, and the establishment of its reality.

The polytheists, the star-worshippers, and the worshippers of other bodies—human or otherwise—used to face their idols and gods, turning toward them with their bodies in close proximity.

However, the religion of the prophets—and we specifically mention among them the religion of Islam, which confirms them all—established the Kaaba as the *qiblah* and commanded facing it in prayer, for which no Muslim is excused, wherever he may be in the regions and horizons of the earth. It forbade facing it or turning one's back to it in certain states and recommended it in others. Thus, it preserved the turning of the human heart toward the House of Allah, ensuring that he does not forget his Lord in his solitude or public life, his standing or sitting, his sleep or wakefulness, his ritual or worship, even in his lowliest and most mundane states. This is from the perspective of the individual.

As for the perspective of society, the matter is more wondrous and the effect more manifest and impactful. It has gathered people, despite their different times and places, to turn toward a single point, representing thereby their intellectual unity, the connection of their community, and the harmony of their hearts. This is the most subtle spirit that can permeate all the affairs of individuals in their material and spiritual vitality. It provides the highest form of society and the most complete and powerful unity. Allah, the Exalted, has favored His Muslim servants with this and preserved through it the unity of their religion and the might of their gathering, even after they split into factions and divided into various sects and divergent paths where no two of them agree on an opinion. We thank Allah, the Exalted, for His favors.
